| `capture` | 3 | 2 | capture health exists but RBAC evaluate/policy are stubbed; capture governance needs real policy enforcement |

#### Account Security UX Redline

Using the `ui-ux-pro-max` checklist, post-auth trust pages must be dense, operational, and failure-aware. A security dashboard cannot rely on marketing language, hover-only disclosure, or static cards.

| account page | current signal | required UX upgrade |
|---|---|---|
| `public/account/api-keys.html` | no live fetch found in scan | show live key list, scopes, created/last-used timestamps, rotation, revoke, copy-once behavior, audit link, empty/error/loading states |
| `public/account/audit-log.html` | no live fetch found, form present, certification terms present | fetch real audit events, filters, export preview, retention state, SIEM copy, and no certification overclaim |
| `public/account/security/2fa.html` | no live fetch found | expose real MFA/WebAuthn enrollment state, recovery codes, enforcement policy, team exceptions, and admin audit events |
| `public/account/continuous-monitoring.html` | no live fetch found, SOC 2 language present | show controls implemented versus certified, monitoring snapshot route status, evidence freshness, and missing proof gates |
| `public/account/sla.html` | no live fetch found, claim terms present | show real uptime/latency/error budget, incident links, region/provider scope, and no unsupported SLA copy |
| `public/account/overview.html` | fetches product graph and closeout ledger, desc length 12 | keep readiness truth spine; fix metadata, make security posture a first-class panel, and link unresolved gates |
| `public/account/privacy-events.html` | fetches sensitive observations | add class counts, policy decisions, redaction benchmark scope, export/delete actions, and PHI/PII warning states |
| `public/account/storage.html` | fetches object readiness and config | keep `secret_values_included: false` visible and add data-region/residency linkage |

#### Possible Secret Literal Triage

The scan flagged key-shaped strings or password-shaped examples in `src/router.js`, `scripts/build-docs-w374.cjs`, `apps/export/ai_act_docs.py`, `src/byoc.js`, `apps/trainer/merge.py`, `scripts/e2e-walk.mjs`, `src/secrets-vault.js`, `scripts/probe-postauth.mjs`, `scripts/probe-pill.mjs`, and `scripts/probe-screenshot.mjs`.

Manual context shows several are false positives such as comments, docs examples, or test keys. The completion rule is still strict:

1. every key-shaped literal must be classified as fake/test/example/real;
2. fake keys must use impossible prefixes or clearly invalid sentinel values;
3. real keys must be rotated, purged, and never written into the blueprint;
4. browser probe scripts must read `KOLM_API_KEY` from environment or a local ignored file, not hardcode tenant-looking values;
5. claim gates must fail if public docs display realistic secret values as copyable examples.

#### Current Security Test And CI Evidence

| evidence area | current signal | gap |
|---|---|---|
| auth tests | `auth.test.js`, `auth-hash.test.js`, hosted auth, chat completions auth, OAuth route contract | expand route-auth matrix so every sensitive route has positive and negative auth coverage |
| tenant isolation | `wave411-tenant-isolation.test.js` and team/admin tests exist | prove object-level authorization across account, teams, captures, artifacts, distill jobs, billing, and admin |
| privacy/redaction | privacy, redaction leak, redaction benchmark, public benchmark contract tests exist | keep public F1/recall claims tied to benchmark date, classes, and fixture scope |
| compliance | compliance packet, regulatory, SOC2/ISO, EU AI Act, audit export, residency tests exist | keep certification claims scoped; implemented controls are not awarded certifications |
| supply chain | `sbom.yml` emits CycloneDX and SPDX fallback; `lint.yml` runs `npm audit --omit=dev --audit-level=high` | add secret scanning, CodeQL or equivalent SAST, dependency review, Scorecard, artifact attestations, and release-blocking SBOM verification |
| workflows | `kolm.yml` and templates use read-only permissions by default and guarded secrets patterns | review every workflow for least privilege, pinned actions, OIDC, provenance, and fork safety |

#### Official Security And Trust Standards

| standard | source | Kolm completion rule |
|---|---|---|
| OWASP API Security Top 10 2023 | OWASP lists API1 broken object-level authorization, API2 broken authentication, API3 broken object property-level authorization, API4 unrestricted resource consumption, API5 broken function-level authorization, API6 sensitive business flows, API7 SSRF, API8 misconfiguration, API9 inventory, API10 unsafe API consumption: https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0x00-header/ | route matrix must prove object-level, property-level, and function-level authorization for every tenant-owned resource |
| OWASP ASVS | OWASP ASVS provides a basis for testing web application technical security controls and a list of secure development requirements: https://owasp.org/www-project-application-security-verification-standard/ | enterprise readiness must map controls to ASVS levels and evidence, not generic "secure" copy |
| OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications | OWASP's LLM project identifies critical vulnerabilities for LLM applications and publishes the latest list: https://owasp.org/www-project-top-10-for-large-language-model-applications/ | capture, distill, redteam, prompt extraction, poisoning, tool-use, and agent surfaces need explicit LLM security gates |
| NIST AI RMF and GenAI Profile | NIST AI RMF 1.0 is voluntary risk guidance; NIST released AI 600-1 for generative AI risk management on July 26, 2024: https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework | product graph and account UI should map AI risks to govern, map, measure, and manage evidence |
| NIST SSDF SP 800-218 | NIST SSDF recommends secure software development practices to reduce vulnerabilities, mitigate exploitation impact, and address root causes: https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/sp/800/218/final | completion requires secure-build evidence, vulnerability response, dependency governance, and release provenance |
| OpenID Connect Core 1.0 | OIDC Core defines authentication built on OAuth 2.0 and claims for communicating end-user information: https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0-final.html | SSO/OAuth flows need state, nonce, redirect allowlist, token handling, claims mapping, session lifecycle, and negative tests |
| SCIM 2.0 | SCIM protocol is RFC 7644: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7644 | SCIM routes must implement standard Users/Groups operations or clearly say partial/service-provider-config-only |
| WebAuthn Level 3 | WebAuthn defines public-key credentials scoped to a relying party for strong user authentication: https://www.w3.org/TR/webauthn-3/ | MFA page should move toward passkey/WebAuthn enrollment, recovery, and admin enforcement proof |
| HIPAA Security Rule | HHS describes HIPAA Security Rule safeguards and security risk assessment responsibilities: https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/security/index.html | health copy should say HIPAA-aligned or BAA support only when evidence exists; do not imply certification |
| GDPR | European Commission data protection guidance covers GDPR and EU data-protection rules: https://commission.europa.eu/law/law-topic/data-protection_en | EU/privacy pages need data subject rights, deletion/export, processor/subprocessor, residency, and pseudonymization caveats |
| EU AI Act | European Commission states the AI Act entered into force on August 1, 2024 and is fully applicable from August 2, 2026 with exceptions: https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/policies/regulatory-framework-ai | AI Act copy must state scope, risk class, evidence generated, and what is not legal certification |

#### Security Completion Contract

Every security-sensitive feature must prove:

1. authentication mode and anonymous allowance, if any;
2. tenant object ownership check;
3. function-level authorization and role requirement;
4. object property filtering before serialization;
5. audit event with actor, tenant, target, action, result, request id, and timestamp;
6. secret-safe response with no raw tokens, no provider secrets, and `secret_values_included: false` where readiness is exposed;
7. validation and error shape with no stack traces;
8. rate limit and abuse control for sensitive business flows;
9. UI loading, empty, error, success, destructive-confirmation, and recovery states;
10. documentation scope label: implemented, simulated, external-gated, certification-gated, or package-release-gated;
11. release proof from tests, local smoke, generated docs, account screenshot, and production smoke.

#### Master Spec Tree Branch 10

Security is cross-cutting enough to get its own branch, while still linking back to the existing product nodes.

| node | branch | owner question | required proof |
|---|---|---|---|
| `10.0` | security inventory | what routes, pages, modules, workflows, and docs are security-sensitive? | generated security surface ledger with route/page/module/workflow ownership |
| `10.1` | authentication | who is the actor and how is the session/API key/OAuth token validated? | auth matrix, token tests, OAuth/OIDC negative tests, session lifecycle |
| `10.2` | authorization | what tenant, object, role, and property checks apply? | BOLA/BFLA/BOPLA tests for every sensitive route |
| `10.3` | secrets | where can keys, provider credentials, and signing material appear? | secret scanner, fake-key policy, rotation playbook, no realistic literals in docs |
| `10.4` | privacy | what PII/PHI/business-sensitive data is captured, transformed, stored, exported, or deleted? | data-class ledger, redaction benchmarks, export/delete tests, privacy UI |
| `10.5` | AI security | how are prompt injection, poisoning, extraction, tool abuse, and unsafe outputs handled? | OWASP LLM mapping, redteam corpus, guardrail tests, account failure-mode display |
| `10.6` | compliance | which controls are implemented and which certifications are external? | evidence packet, closeout ledger, no overclaim lint, account trust UX |
| `10.7` | supply chain | can release artifacts be traced and verified? | SBOM, SLSA/provenance, attestations, dependency review, pinned workflows |
| `10.8` | incident response | what happens when security controls fail? | incident playbook, audit export, customer notification workflow, test drill |
| `10.9` | trust UX | can a buyer understand current posture in under 5 seconds? | account security dashboard, trust page, scope chips, missing-proof states, screenshots |

#### Security/Auth/Privacy/Compliance Tickets To Generate

- W-SEC-001: generated security surface ledger for routes, pages, modules, workflows, docs, data classes, and owners.
- W-SEC-002: route auth matrix with anonymous/API key/session/admin/team role requirements and negative tests.
- W-SEC-003: object-level and function-level authorization test pack for account, teams, captures, artifacts, jobs, billing, admin, marketplace, and storage.
- W-SEC-004: secret literal classifier and scanner gate for source, public docs, generated examples, probe scripts, and screenshots.
- W-SEC-005: API key UX and backend contract: scopes, last used, rotation, revoke, copy-once, audit link, and recovery.
- W-SEC-006: MFA/WebAuthn account page and backend contract, including passkey enrollment, recovery codes, enforcement, and audit events.
- W-SEC-007: SSO/OIDC route and UI contract with state/nonce/redirect/token/claims negative tests.
- W-SEC-008: SCIM completion or scope demotion for Users/Groups CRUD, filtering, pagination, errors, and provisioning audit.
- W-SEC-009: privacy data-class ledger connected to redaction benchmark, capture review, deletion/export, and account privacy events.
- W-SEC-010: AI security mapping for OWASP LLM risks across capture poisoning, prompt extraction, tool use, agent registry, redteam, and output validation.
- W-SEC-011: compliance claim-scope rewrite for SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, GDPR, EU AI Act, BAA, DPA, and FedRAMP-adjacent language.
- W-SEC-012: supply-chain workflow hardening: CodeQL or equivalent SAST, secret scanning, dependency review, Scorecard, action pinning, OIDC provenance, SBOM verification, and artifact attestations.
- W-SEC-013: trust/account UX redesign for security posture, implemented controls, missing proof gates, evidence freshness, and customer export.
- W-SEC-014: incident-response and audit-export product path with drill fixtures, customer notification templates, and SIEM-ready logs.
- W-SEC-015: production security smoke pack that runs after deploy with both candidate `KOLM_API_KEY` values only from environment, never hardcoded in repo files.

#### Node 10 And Node 20 Credential Lifecycle Addendum V2

This addendum owns the credential and secret lifecycle across master nodes `10.1`, `10.3`, `20.3`, `29.2`, `29.4`, and `35.1`. It is intentionally not a new node: authentication, secret storage, environment parity, production smoke, and provider credential readiness already have owners. The missing piece is a single end-to-end credential contract that covers creation, display, storage, use, audit, rotation, revocation, leak response, and post-auth UX.

Operational rule: any real-looking API key, admin key, provider key, OAuth secret, receipt secret, storage secret, or webhook secret pasted into chat, tickets, screenshots, docs, logs, prompts, browser storage, or public examples must be treated as exposed until a rotation record proves otherwise. The blueprint must never store the literal value.

Current repo evidence:

| Evidence | Current state observed | Completion gap |
|---|---|---|
| `src/auth.js` key storage | Tenant keys are minted with `ks_` or `kao_`, stored as SHA-256 hashes with prefixes, compared with timing-safe equality, and legacy plaintext columns are migrated away. | Key hashing is good, but a fast unsalted hash of high-entropy random keys still needs a formal threat model, key-length entropy statement, leak-response policy, and scanner proof. |
| `src/auth.js` request auth | Auth reads httpOnly cookie, `Authorization: Bearer`, and `X-API-Key`; query-param `api_key` is rejected to avoid log/referrer leakage. | Every protected route needs generated auth-mode proof, and bearer-token behavior needs a public contract aligned with RFC 6750. |
| `src/auth.js` rotation | `rotateTenantKey()` invalidates the tenant primary key; OAuth sign-in can rotate the key for the browser session. | Product copy and account UI must explain primary-key rotation accurately; true multi-key support is not shipped until a dedicated key table exists. |
| `src/router.js` account key routes | `/v1/account/keys` list/create/revoke exists, but comments state the tenant model holds one `api_key_hash` and returns a one-row table. | `public/account/api-keys.html` currently markets individual scoped keys and revoke-by-prefix; until multi-key lands, UI/docs/CLI must say "primary tenant key" or implement real multi-key scopes. |
| `public/account/api-keys.html` | Fetches `/v1/account/keys`, supports rotate/create/revoke UI, and says full secret is shown once. | It reads keys from several `localStorage` names, falls back to rendering the current localStorage key as a row, uses browser alerts for new secrets, and displays scope references that are not proven by backend per-key scopes. |
| `public/account/audit-log.html` | Documents key.created/key.rotated/key.revoked and fetches account audit log. | Audit event names, backend `AUDIT_OPS`, UI filters, and CLI audit output must be made canonical; every key create/rotate/revoke must produce an exportable event without raw secrets. |
| `public/account/security/2fa.html` | States hosted accounts use IdP MFA and scoped API keys, and links key rotation. | MFA/WebAuthn and scoped key claims need live backend state or visible external/IdP-managed scope labels. |
| `src/oauth.js` | Uses state cookies, httpOnly secure cookies in production, safe return path, and provider token exchange. | OAuth security should be upgraded to RFC 9700 expectations: exact redirect matching, PKCE where applicable, nonce/session binding for OIDC, and token-error redaction tests. |
| `src/secrets-vault.js` | Local AES-256-GCM vault with 0600 key file, external ref prefixes, secret reference resolution, and redacted envelopes. | The local vault is not a production secret manager; production profiles need external secret-manager contracts, envelope encryption/KMS notes, rotation, backup/restore, and access audit. |
| `scripts/sensitive-data-readiness.sh` | Runs a production-oriented tenant-isolation/auth/header/error-leak probe. | It uses shell parsing and creates tenants on the target; it needs a safe release-smoke mode, JSON report, cleanup, rate limiting, and integration into release-control-room evidence. |
| `secret_values_included:false` pattern | Many routes/tests assert secret-safe readiness outputs. | This must become a generated invariant: every readiness/config/provider/storage/cloud response has an allowlisted shape, no raw env values, no key-shaped strings, and a test fixture. |
| Encoding and comments | Some auth/env/source comments show encoding artifacts in current output. | Security-critical files must be mojibake-free so operators do not misread key lifecycle, sanctions, OAuth, and recovery comments. |

External credential baseline:

| Source | Kolm rule |
|---|---|
| OWASP Secrets Management Cheat Sheet, `https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Secrets_Management_Cheat_Sheet.html` | Secrets need a lifecycle: creation, storage, access, rotation, revocation, auditing, and automation through CI/CD or a secret-management system. Kolm must not rely on ad hoc env docs or browser storage as the source of truth. |
| OWASP API Security Top 10 2023, `https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0x00-header/` | Broken authentication and authorization are core API risks. Every API-key route, tenant resource, and provider credential route needs auth, object ownership, role, property filtering, rate limiting, and negative tests. |
| OWASP Authentication Cheat Sheet, `https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Authentication_Cheat_Sheet.html` | Authentication systems need secure credential handling, recovery, rate limiting, logging, and MFA guidance. Kolm account pages must show actual MFA/session/key state or clearly label IdP-managed state. |
| OWASP Session Management Cheat Sheet, `https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Session_Management_Cheat_Sheet.html` | Browser storage like localStorage is accessible to scripts from the same origin. Kolm must avoid long-lived API keys in localStorage for production account UX, or visibly scope that path as local/developer fallback. |
| NIST SP 800-63B, `https://pages.nist.gov/800-63-4/sp800-63b.html` | Authentication secrets need sufficient entropy, throttling, protected channels, and lifecycle management. Kolm must document key entropy, generation, verification, rate limits, replay behavior, and recovery. |
| RFC 6750 Bearer Token Usage, `https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6750` | Bearer tokens grant access to whoever possesses them and must be protected in storage and transport. Kolm API keys must be treated as bearer secrets: no URLs, no logs, no screenshots, no public docs, and immediate rotation on exposure. |
| RFC 9700 OAuth 2.0 Security BCP, `https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9700` | Modern OAuth requires stronger redirect validation and PKCE for public clients. Kolm OAuth should prove state, redirect allowlist, PKCE/nonce where relevant, cookie flags, token exchange errors, and no token leakage. |
| GitHub secret scanning and push protection, `https://docs.github.com/en/code-security/secret-scanning/enabling-secret-scanning-features` | Secret scanning should detect existing exposed secrets and push protection should prevent new leaks. Kolm needs repo-wide scanners for source, docs, screenshots, generated HTML, reports, and examples. |

Credential lifecycle contract:

```json
{
  "schema": "kolm-credential-lifecycle-v2",
  "credential_classes": [
    "tenant_api_key",
    "anonymous_api_key",
    "admin_key",
    "receipt_secret",
    "tenant_receipt_secret",
    "oauth_client_secret",
    "stripe_secret",
    "webhook_secret",
    "provider_api_key",
    "object_storage_secret",
    "trainer_bridge_token",
    "local_vault_key",
    "external_secret_ref"
  ],
  "requirements": {
    "generation": "cryptographically_random_with_documented_entropy",
    "storage": "hashed_or_encrypted_or_external_ref",
    "display": "copy_once_or_prefix_only",
    "transport": "authorization_header_or_http_only_cookie_only",
    "browser_storage": "not_allowed_for_long_lived_production_keys",
    "logging": "prefix_only_no_raw_secret",
    "audit": "actor_tenant_action_target_result_request_id_timestamp",
    "rotation": "self_service_and_emergency_paths",
    "revocation": "immediate_for_tenant_api_keys",
    "leak_response": "quarantine_rotate_purge_reissue_notify",
    "readiness": "secret_values_included_false",
    "docs": "fake_sentinel_values_only"
  },
  "proof": {
    "route_auth_matrix": "required",
    "secret_scanner": "required",
    "account_screenshots": "required",
    "cli_parity": "required",
    "production_smoke": "required"
  }
}
```

Credential UX rules:

1. Account pages must use a single auth accessor that prefers httpOnly session cookies for browser use and avoids storing long-lived `ks_` keys in localStorage for production sessions.
2. If localStorage fallback remains for developer/local mode, it must be visibly labeled as local/dev fallback and excluded from enterprise security claims.
3. Newly created keys must appear in a secure copy-once UI with no browser alert, no URL, no history entry, no analytics event, no screenshot in automated reports, and a "copied / not copied" state.
4. A key list must only show prefix, last four, label, scope, created time, last used time, last used IP/location class if available, owner, status, and audit link.
5. If the backend has only one primary tenant key, the UI must not show "individual keys", "revoke one key", or per-key scopes as shipped functionality.
6. If real multi-key support ships, each key needs its own row, hash, prefix, label, scope set, owner, created/last-used metadata, revocation timestamp, and audit trail.
7. Scopes must be enforced in middleware before route handlers, not just displayed in docs.
8. Destructive credential actions need confirmation, post-action verification, rollback/recovery guidance, and audit export.
9. Readiness pages must show configured/missing/provider class without raw values and with `secret_values_included:false` visible.
10. Support and sales workflows must instruct users to rotate any key pasted into chat, email, ticket, screenshots, logs, or issue reports.

Credential lifecycle work tickets:

| Ticket | Branch | Work | Done when |
|---|---|---|---|
| `W-CRED-001` | `10.0` | Generate a credential inventory. | Every API key, admin key, OAuth secret, Stripe key, webhook secret, receipt secret, storage secret, provider credential, local vault key, and external secret ref has owner, source file, env var, storage mode, display policy, rotation path, and smoke proof. |
| `W-CRED-002` | `10.1` | Formalize bearer-token contract. | Auth docs, OpenAPI, middleware tests, SDK examples, and account copy agree on `Authorization: Bearer`, `X-API-Key`, httpOnly cookie, rejected query params, 401 shape, and rate limits. |
| `W-CRED-003` | `10.1` | Add key entropy and prefix proof. | Tenant and anonymous key generators have documented entropy, format, collision analysis, invalid-prefix tests, unknown-key tests, and no provider-key confusion. |
| `W-CRED-004` | `10.3` | Build the secret leak classifier. | Source, docs, public HTML, generated assets, reports, screenshots, examples, fixtures, prompts, and scripts classify key-shaped strings as fake/test/example/real with rotation-required status. |
| `W-CRED-005` | `10.3` | Add impossible fake-key policy. | Public examples use impossible sentinel values that cannot authenticate and cannot be confused with real `ks_`, provider, Stripe, OAuth, or storage secrets. |
| `W-CRED-006` | `10.3` | Add leak-response playbook. | A pasted or detected key triggers rotate, revoke, purge logs/artifacts, notify owner, reissue, audit, and post-incident evidence without copying the secret into the report. |
| `W-CRED-007` | `7.0` | Fix account API-key truth. | `/account/api-keys` either states "primary tenant key" everywhere or the backend ships real multi-key rows with enforced scopes. |
| `W-CRED-008` | `7.0` | Remove production localStorage key dependency. | Post-auth account pages use httpOnly session cookies or a safe account auth bridge; localStorage API key fallback is dev/local-scoped and visually labeled. |
| `W-CRED-009` | `7.0` | Replace alert-based secret display. | Create/rotate flows show copy-once secret panels with copy state, timeout/masking, no analytics, no screenshots, and explicit "store in secrets manager" instruction. |
| `W-CRED-010` | `10.2` | Enforce key scopes. | Read/write/compile/capture/admin scopes are middleware-enforced, tested route by route, visible in OpenAPI, and logged in audit events. |
| `W-CRED-011` | `10.2` | Add key owner and device metadata. | Key rows include owner/member/service account, label, created source, last used timestamp, last used route class, and revocation actor. |
| `W-CRED-012` | `10.8` | Canonicalize audit events. | `key.created`, `key.rotated`, `key.revoked`, OAuth sign-in, session creation, secret-vault access, and provider credential changes share canonical event names and export shapes. |
| `W-CRED-013` | `20.3` | Upgrade production secret-manager profiles. | AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, Azure Key Vault, Vault, 1Password, Doppler, and Infisical refs have configure, resolve, rotate, readiness, and failure-mode contracts. |
| `W-CRED-014` | `20.3` | Scope local vault correctly. | `src/secrets-vault.js` is documented as local/dev/self-host starter unless backed by KMS/external manager, with backup, restore, chmod, and key-loss behavior. |
| `W-CRED-015` | `29.2` | Add secret-safe env parity report. | Release packets compare required/optional/deprecated env vars by presence and class only, never value, across local, CI, preview, production, Railway, Vercel, Docker, worker, and airgap. |
| `W-CRED-016` | `29.4` | Add production credential smoke. | Production smoke proves auth, bogus keys, expired anon keys, query-param rejection, object storage readiness, provider readiness, and account key pages without printing secrets. |
| `W-CRED-017` | `10.1` | Upgrade OAuth security proof. | OAuth routes prove state, redirect allowlist, exact callback URL, PKCE or documented server-side exception, OIDC nonce where relevant, cookie flags, token redaction, and negative tests. |
| `W-CRED-018` | `10.3` | Block secrets in screenshots and reports. | Screenshot audits and reports redact or skip copy-once key panels and assert no key-shaped strings in generated PNG metadata, HTML reports, logs, or markdown. |
| `W-CRED-019` | `8.0` | Fix docs and CLI examples. | Docs, CLI help, OpenAPI examples, tutorials, and generated snippets never include realistic secrets, stale prefixes, or "paste key into browser" instructions without scope labels. |
| `W-CRED-020` | `29.5` | Add credential incident drill. | A simulated leaked tenant key proves detection, emergency rotation, old-key rejection, audit export, customer notice decision, support script, and release-control-room attachment. |

Credential relax redline:

- Can the product prove which credential classes exist and where each can appear?
- Can every credential be rotated or revoked by a documented owner without exposing the value?
- Can a user understand whether they are managing one primary tenant key or multiple scoped keys?
- Can every account/API/CLI/docs surface avoid storing or rendering raw long-lived keys outside copy-once flows?
- Can production smoke prove valid key, invalid key, expired key, query-param rejection, rate limit, and secret-free readiness behavior?
- Can a leaked key be detected, rotated, purged from evidence, and documented without pasting the key into the incident packet?
- Can every provider/storage/cloud readiness response prove `secret_values_included:false` with an allowlisted schema?

If any answer is no, credential lifecycle remains open even when broad auth tests pass.

#### Node 10 V2 Security, Auth, Privacy, Compliance, And Trust Evidence Addendum

This V2 addendum keeps the master spec tree intact: node `52` remains unused because security already belongs to node `10`. The active correction is stricter than the V1 ledger. Node `10` is not a trust page, a compliance packet, or an auth middleware file. It is the security control system for the entire product: every model call, capture event, redaction decision, artifact, distill job, device deployment, team action, billing action, provider credential, storage credential, admin operation, compliance statement, audit export, and production smoke must have an explicit control owner and evidence.

This is a research/specification edit only. It does not modify auth middleware, routes, UI, docs generators, tests, scanners, workflows, or public pages while parallel implementation agents are active.

Current repo evidence checked for this pass:

| Evidence class | Current evidence | Node `10` completion implication |
|---|---:|---|
| API route inventory | `public/docs/api-routes.json` reports 582 total routes and 163 groups. A security-term pass matched 259 routes across 113 groups, with 19 matched stubs. | Security cannot be reviewed from `/security` pages alone. A generated route-control matrix must own auth mode, tenant fence, role, data class, audit event, rate limit, secret policy, and UI/docs exposure for every matched route. |
| Security implementation modules | 17 primary modules, 259,984 bytes, 6,024 lines across auth, audit, keys, OAuth, privacy, redaction, secret vault, tenant holdout, team RBAC, public key directory, and compliance packet code. | The implementation is real and broad, but completion needs one generated control catalog and one proof graph, not scattered confidence in large modules. |
| Security-related tests | 53 matched test files, 689,671 bytes. Coverage includes auth hash, OAuth route contract, tenant isolation, privacy fail-closed, redaction leaks, redaction benchmark, keys route docs, compliance packet, SOC2/ISO, SBOM, audit export, and dependency audit. | Test presence is strong, but finality requires generated route-to-test mapping and negative tests for every sensitive route, not only named wave tests. |
| Public trust/security pages | 18 focused pages, 427,445 bytes. Every sampled page has one H1 and JSON-LD schema. | Trust content exists, but it must converge into one evidence hierarchy with implemented controls, live certifications, closeout gates, and production freshness. |
| Public trust page polish debt | Focused trust pages still show 39 mojibake markers and 355 certification/compliance terms. | Trust pages are not done until browser titles, schema, copy, and visual states are clean and never imply awarded certifications without proof. |
| Live certification state | `public/product-readiness-closeout.json` still records `needs_live_certification` for `compliance-certifications`. | Security controls can be implemented locally, but SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA BAA, GDPR DPA, FedRAMP boundary, SLSA/SBOM evidence, and auditor reports remain external proof gates until dated artifacts exist. |
| KMS truth | `src/keys.js` supports local key rotation and KMS export/rotation intents for AWS KMS, GCP KMS, Azure Key Vault, Vault, and local, with hosted KMS status `awaiting_operator_hook`. | Product copy must not say "KMS integrated" when Kolm emits import/wrap intent and a customer or operator hook applies it. The API/UI must expose `api_status` honestly. |
| Auth truth | `src/auth.js` stores tenant API keys as SHA-256 hashes with prefixes, compares hashes timing-safely, migrates legacy plaintext keys away, supports bearer/header/cookie auth, rejects query-param API keys, rate limits per tenant, tracks quota, provisions anon tenants, supports OAuth tenants, rotates tenant keys, and publishes SAML/SCIM metadata-only public allowlist entries. | The baseline is credible, but finality requires a route-auth allowlist verifier, key entropy statement, session lifecycle contract, OAuth/OIDC negative tests, scoped-key truth, and browser credential-storage redline. |
| OAuth truth | `src/oauth.js` implements Google and GitHub OAuth with state/return cookies, provider token exchange, userinfo fetch, tenant lookup/create, httpOnly session cookie, and configured redirect base. | OAuth must graduate from "works" to "OIDC-grade proof": exact redirect allowlist, state binding, nonce where OIDC claims are used, token-error redaction, cookie flag tests, replay tests, and production callback smoke. |
| Audit truth | `src/audit.js` implements append-only per-tenant HMAC audit chains and `verifyAuditChain(tenant_id)`. Account and audit routes expose audit log/export/verify paths. | Audit is a moat only if every security-sensitive action emits canonical actor/action/object/result/request/event data and the customer can verify chain integrity after export. |
| Privacy/redaction truth | `src/privacy-membrane.js`, `src/phi-redactor.js`, `src/prompt-redactor.js`, `src/redaction-benchmark.js`, and `src/services/redactor.js` implement redaction and privacy paths with benchmark tests. | Redaction claims must show class coverage, benchmark date, precision/recall scope, false-positive/false-negative examples, and what is not certified. |
| Secret-vault truth | `src/secrets-vault.js` provides a local AES-256-GCM vault, external secret refs, redacted envelopes, and reference resolution. | The local vault is useful but not enough for enterprise production claims. Production profiles need external secret manager contracts, KMS/envelope encryption scope, backup/restore, rotation, audit, and readiness smoke. |
| Account trust UX | Node `7` already owns post-auth account UX; node `10` owns the security semantics behind account pages such as audit log, API keys, 2FA, privacy events, storage, continuous monitoring, compliance package, and readiness closeout. | Account security pages must show live posture, data source, freshness, missing proof, error state, action, export, and no hidden certification caveat. |

##### Node 10 V2 External Baselines

These are the current external baselines this branch should track in generated evidence. The implementation agent must cite exact versions in the release packet because some frameworks are evolving.

| Baseline | Source | Kolm completion rule |
|---|---|---|
| OWASP ASVS | `https://owasp.org/www-project-application-security-verification-standard/` | Map web/API technical controls to ASVS requirements where applicable. Do not claim "enterprise secure" without mapped evidence for auth, session, access control, validation, errors, files, SSRF, config, and API behavior. |
| OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025 | `https://owasp.org/www-project-top-10-for-large-language-model-applications/` | Capture, distill, agent, tool, redteam, prompt extraction, model supply-chain, data poisoning, sensitive disclosure, excessive agency, and model theft controls need explicit tests and account-visible status. |
| NIST AI RMF 1.0 and GenAI Profile | `https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework` | Product graph, K-Score, redteam, eval, drift, benchmark, and governance views must map AI risks to govern, map, measure, and manage evidence. GenAI-specific risks must be connected to the redline and not reduced to generic "AI safe" copy. |
| NIST Privacy Framework | `https://www.nist.gov/privacy-framework` | Privacy completion requires data inventory, processing purpose, data subject rights, consent, retention, deletion, redaction, privacy events, and customer export. Track final framework version and do not silently mix draft and final controls. |
| NIST SSDF SP 800-218 | `https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/sp/800/218/final` | Secure development proof must include protected source, dependency risk, vulnerability response, review evidence, release provenance, and incident remediation. Node `9` owns release proof; node `10` owns security control evidence. |
| RFC 7644 SCIM | `https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7644` | SCIM must implement or explicitly scope ServiceProviderConfig, Users, Groups, filtering, pagination, schemas, errors, auth, provisioning audit, and deprovisioning behavior. Metadata-only cannot be sold as full SCIM. |
| OpenID Connect Core 1.0 | `https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0-final.html` | OAuth/OIDC must prove identity claims, redirect/callback handling, state/nonce, token handling, session creation, logout/revocation behavior, cookie flags, and negative tests. |
| OASIS SAML 2.0 metadata | `https://docs.oasis-open.org/security/saml/Post2.0/sstc-metadata-iop-os.html` | SAML metadata can be public, but tenant SSO configuration, certificate handling, ACS validation, attribute mapping, and deprovisioning remain auth-gated enterprise controls. |

##### Node 10 V2 Security Control Contract

Every security-sensitive surface must be representable by a machine-readable contract before the build is final:

```json
{
  "schema": "kolm-security-control-contract-v2",
  "route": "GET /v1/example",
  "node": "10",
  "owner": "security-auth-privacy-compliance",
  "auth": {
    "mode": "none|api_key|session|oauth|admin|service|invite_token|public_metadata",
    "anonymous_allowed": false,
    "public_allowlist_reason": "not_public_unless_named",
    "negative_tests": ["missing_credential", "wrong_tenant", "expired_key", "wrong_role"]
  },
  "authorization": {
    "tenant_fence": "required",
    "object_owner_check": "required",
    "role_or_scope": "required",
    "property_filtering": "required_before_serialization"
  },
  "data": {
    "classes": ["none", "tenant_metadata", "api_key_prefix", "provider_secret_ref", "pii", "phi", "artifact", "audit_event"],
    "retention": "linked_policy_id",
    "exportable": true,
    "deletable": true
  },
  "secrets": {
    "raw_secret_returned": "never_except_copy_once_creation",
    "secret_values_included": false,
    "kms_status": "local_applied|awaiting_operator_hook|external_manager|not_applicable"
  },
  "ai_security": {
    "owasp_llm_risks": ["prompt_injection", "sensitive_information_disclosure", "supply_chain"],
    "guardrail_tests": "required_when_ai_io_present",
    "redaction_policy": "required_when_user_or_patient_data_present"
  },
  "audit": {
    "event_name": "canonical.required",
    "fields": ["actor", "tenant", "action", "object", "result", "request_id", "timestamp"],
    "chain_verifiable": true
  },
  "ui_and_docs": {
    "account_entry": "required_if_post_auth",
    "docs_entry": "required_if_public_api",
    "claim_scope": "implemented|partial|external_gated|certification_gated"
  },
  "proof": {
    "unit_tests": [],
    "integration_tests": [],
    "local_smoke": [],
    "prod_smoke": [],
    "screenshots": [],
    "evidence_paths": []
  }
}
```

##### Node 10 V2 Proof Classes

| Proof class | Meaning | Required before "done" |
|---|---|---|
| `source-present` | Code exists for the control. | Never enough alone. |
| `unit-tested` | Direct unit tests cover the control. | Required for parsing, crypto, redaction, auth helpers, and policy helpers. |
| `route-enforced` | Route middleware or handler enforces auth, tenant, role, validation, and response filtering. | Required for every non-public route. |
| `negative-tested` | Missing/wrong/expired credentials, wrong tenant, wrong role, invalid body, and leak attempts fail safely. | Required for every sensitive route. |
| `tenant-isolation-proven` | Cross-tenant reads/writes are blocked and object IDs cannot bypass tenant fence. | Required for account, teams, capture, artifacts, jobs, billing, admin, storage, and audit. |
| `secret-safe` | No raw secrets appear in logs, readiness outputs, docs, screenshots, reports, JSON examples, or errors. | Required for any provider/storage/cloud/credential feature. |
| `privacy-fail-closed` | Redaction/privacy gates block or quarantine on unknown/risky input instead of silently passing. | Required for capture, privacy lake, media, healthcare, and compliance workflows. |
| `audit-verifiable` | Action emits a canonical audit event and the event can be exported and chain-verified. | Required for security, billing, governance, key, team, data, artifact, and admin actions. |
| `ai-threat-tested` | AI-specific threats are mapped and exercised against fixtures or redteam cases. | Required for capture, distill, agents, tool use, output validation, prompt extraction, poisoning, and model access. |
| `compliance-evidence-linked` | Control maps to claim, evidence path, freshness, owner, and external gate status. | Required for trust pages and enterprise exports. |
| `production-security-smoked` | Deployed origin proves auth, public allowlist, invalid-key rejection, account security pages, readiness secret safety, and audit export. | Required before production-complete claims. |
| `external-certified` | Dated auditor/certification/package/partner evidence exists. | Required before saying certified, audited, listed, published, or partner-supported. |

##### Node 10 V2 Route-Control Redline

The current scan matched 259 security-related routes. That broad match includes some routes where security is indirect, but it is still the correct default because model traffic, artifacts, evals, provider config, and account actions are security-sensitive by design.

The route-control ledger must be generated from `public/docs/api-routes.json`, `src/router.js`, `src/auth.js`, route comments, OpenAPI, account links, docs, and tests. It must fail if any route has one of these states:

1. route accepts tenant-owned input but lacks a declared tenant fence;
2. route reads or mutates tenant-owned objects but lacks negative cross-tenant test;
3. route is public but lacks public allowlist reason;
4. route returns readiness/config/provider data without `secret_values_included:false` or an equivalent allowlisted shape;
5. route is marked as enterprise/security/compliance but is stubbed without visible closeout state;
6. route is admin-only but lacks admin proof and audit event;
7. route accepts body fields that can override tenant, account, provider, storage, team, or artifact ownership;
8. route serializes object properties before filtering by role/scope;
9. route has compliance, privacy, audit, key, or redaction copy but no account or docs entry;
10. route is represented in docs/OpenAPI but not in the security control contract.

##### Node 10 V2 Public Trust And Compliance Page Redline

The focused trust/security page pass found these pages as immediate node `10` assets:

`public/account/audit-log.html`, `public/account/privacy-events.html`, `public/account/security/2fa.html`, `public/audit-log.html`, `public/baa.html`, `public/compliance-packs.html`, `public/dpa.html`, `public/hipaa-mapping.html`, `public/privacy.html`, `public/sbom.html`, `public/security.html`, `public/security/sbom.html`, `public/security/soc2-type2.html`, `public/security/threat-model.html`, `public/slsa.html`, `public/soc2.html`, `public/threat-model.html`, and `public/trust.html`.

Completion rules for those pages:

1. no mojibake in titles, headings, schema, OpenGraph, body copy, or generated snippets;
2. no "certified", "audited", "SOC 2 Type II", "ISO 27001", "FedRAMP", "HIPAA compliant", or equivalent claim unless linked to dated evidence or explicitly scoped as readiness/control mapping;
3. each compliance page states current scope, external blocker, evidence packet path, owner, and next proof step;
4. every security page links to account proof where a logged-in customer can see live state;
5. every public trust page has a machine-readable schema type, canonical URL, fresh title, clear description, and no duplicated stale brand tokens;
6. controls implemented locally are separated from certifications awarded externally;
7. BAA/DPA/legal templates are distinguished from executed agreements;
8. SBOM/SLSA/provenance pages link the current generated artifact, not stale narrative;
9. threat-model pages map threats to controls, tests, owner, residual risk, and last review date;
10. production screenshot and source checks prove trust pages render correctly in light/dark and desktop/mobile.

##### Node 10 V2 Account Trust UX Contract

Using the `ui-ux-pro-max` checklist, account trust UX must act like an operational console, not a brochure. The page should be dense, calm, scannable, and explicit about scope.

Each post-auth security/privacy/compliance screen must show:

1. live status and data source;
2. freshness timestamp and request ID;
3. whether the state is implemented, configured, missing, externally gated, or certification gated;
4. the current tenant/team scope;
5. the next safest action;
6. export/download action where useful;
7. CLI/API equivalent;
8. no raw secret values;
9. no long paragraphs where chips, tables, and direct controls would be clearer;
10. loading, empty, error, degraded, unauthorized, and success states;
11. audit link for every destructive or security-sensitive action;
12. support-safe copy that tells users to rotate secrets if they pasted them anywhere.

| `W-SECURITY-V2-002` | `10.0` | Add a security route-control verifier. | The verifier fails on missing auth mode, public allowlist reason, tenant fence, role/scope rule, audit event, data class, secret policy, or test link. |
| `W-SECURITY-V2-003` | `10.1` | Formalize API key and session contract. | Bearer header, `X-API-Key`, httpOnly session cookie, rejected query params, key entropy, hash strategy, prefixes, quota, rate limit, and error shape are tested and documented. |

| `W-SECURITY-V2-008` | `10.2` | Canonicalize team/RBAC policy. | Team roles, invites, approvals, capture RBAC, billing/admin roles, service accounts, and API-key scopes are represented in one policy table and enforced in middleware or route adapters. |

| `W-SECURITY-V2-012` | `10.4` | Build privacy data-class ledger. | Every capture, prompt, media, label, benchmark, eval, artifact, audit, support, billing, and account field has data class, purpose, retention, export, deletion, redaction, and residency mapping. |
| `W-SECURITY-V2-013` | `10.4` | Upgrade redaction benchmark evidence. | Public redaction reports show classes, fixture date, precision, recall, F1, false-positive examples, false-negative examples, limits, and account-visible benchmark scope. |
| `W-SECURITY-V2-014` | `10.5` | Map OWASP LLM risks to controls. | Prompt injection, insecure output handling, poisoning, model DoS, supply chain, sensitive disclosure, tool/plugin risk, excessive agency, overreliance, and model theft each have tests, owner, UI status, and docs. |

| `W-SECURITY-V2-016` | `10.6` | Rebuild compliance evidence packet around live scope. | SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA BAA, GDPR DPA, FedRAMP boundary, SLSA, SBOM, subprocessors, data residency, controls, tests, and external blockers export as one customer packet. |

| `W-SECURITY-V2-019` | `10.8` | Add incident and leak response drill. | Simulated leaked tenant key, provider key, OAuth secret, webhook secret, bad redaction, and cross-tenant report produce rotate/revoke/quarantine/audit/customer-notice evidence. |
| `W-SECURITY-V2-020` | `10.8` | Canonicalize audit events and SIEM export. | Security, billing, team, key, privacy, compliance, provider, artifact, admin, and incident events share names, fields, filters, export formats, retention, and chain-verification proof. |

| `W-SECURITY-V2-023` | `10.9` | Add buyer trust summary. | `/trust` and account overview answer in under five seconds: what controls are implemented, what is externally gated, what evidence exists, and what an auditor can export. |

##### Node 10 V2 Relax Redline

Node `10` is not done until all of the following are true:

1. every security-sensitive route has a generated control record with auth mode, public allowlist reason if public, tenant fence, role/scope, data class, secret policy, audit event, tests, and UI/docs links;
2. every matched stub in security, compliance, SCIM, SSO, audit, residency, redteam, capture RBAC, and related surfaces is either fully implemented or visibly gated with closeout state;
3. API keys, sessions, OAuth, SSO, SCIM, MFA/passkeys, tenant keys, receipt secrets, provider credentials, storage secrets, webhook secrets, and local vault keys each have lifecycle proof;
4. no raw secret, key-shaped realistic example, provider credential, OAuth token, webhook secret, storage secret, or copied customer key appears in source, docs, public HTML, generated assets, screenshots, logs, reports, or release packets;
5. every readiness/config/provider response that mentions secrets proves `secret_values_included:false` or an equivalent allowlisted schema;
6. object-level, function-level, and property-level authorization are negative-tested across account, teams, admin, capture, artifacts, jobs, storage, billing, marketplace, audit, and provider routes;
7. every PII/PHI/business-sensitive data path has data-class, redaction, retention, export, deletion, consent, residency, and audit treatment;
8. AI security risks from OWASP LLM are mapped to capture, distill, agent, tool, redteam, output-validation, model-access, and benchmark surfaces;
9. audit events are canonical, chain-verifiable, exportable, SIEM-ready, and emitted for every sensitive action;
10. trust/security pages are mojibake-free, scope-accurate, metadata-clean, evidence-linked, screenshot-verified, and do not imply awarded certifications unless external proof exists;
11. compliance packets distinguish implemented controls from live certifications, executed agreements, published packages, public benchmarks, and external partner adoption;
12. account trust UX shows live status, missing prerequisites, freshness, next actions, export paths, CLI/API parity, and safe error states instead of static trust claims;
13. supply-chain gates include dependency risk, SAST, secret scanning, workflow permissions, action pinning, SBOM, provenance, artifact attestations, and release proof;
14. production security smoke runs against the deployed commit with environment-provided keys only and attaches results to the release packet;
15. an incident drill proves detection, containment, rotation, purge, customer communication decision, audit export, and postmortem evidence.

If any item fails, Kolm can have strong security work in progress, but node `10` cannot be called 100 percent complete.

#### Node 10 V3 Trust Boundary, Credential, Compliance, And Production Security Contract

This pass keeps the work inside existing node `10`. It does not create node `52`, and it does not move release gating away from node `9`. Node `10` owns the security truth itself: authentication, authorization, tenancy, credential lifecycle, privacy/redaction, audit events, compliance evidence, public trust claims, and production security smoke. Node `9` owns the decision that the proof is green.

The V3 correction is direct: the codebase has meaningful security implementation and tests, but public trust copy can still contradict the live evidence ledger. A trust system is only state-of-art when the controls, tests, account UI, public pages, compliance packets, and release gates all agree.

##### Node 10.0 Current Trust Evidence Delta V3

| evidence lane | current result | completion implication |
|---|---:|---|
| security-relevant source files | at least 21 top-level `src` files matched auth, privacy, audit, compliance, tokenization, tenant, runtime policy, and secret-vault names | security is distributed; a single trust page cannot be the source of truth |
| focused auth/privacy/tenant/compliance tests | 42/42 passed across auth fallback, OAuth route contract, tenant isolation, privacy membrane, and compliance certification packet tests | strong local behavior evidence exists for selected critical paths |
| compliance packet gate | pass as local contract, but `live_certification_verified=false`, 6 controls, 8/8 files present, 7 blockers | local compliance evidence is real, but live certification and signed external artifacts are not complete |
| compliance blockers | missing `reports/compliance-certification-manifest.json`; missing SOC 2 auditor report, ISO 27001 certificate, signed BAA/counsel packet, GDPR DPA legal review, FedRAMP boundary authorization, signed release provenance | compliance claims must be scoped to implemented controls and available documents until blockers close |
| public trust/security pages | `baa.html`, `compliance-packs.html`, `dpa.html`, `hipaa-mapping.html`, `legal.html`, `legal-v2.html`, `privacy.html`, `security.html`, `soc2.html`, `subprocessors.html`, `trust.html` are present | public trust surface is broad enough to need generated claim-scope and evidence freshness, not copy review by memory |
| claim-scope current failure | `public/security.html:159` says `SOC 2 Type I attested`; additional security page copy states reports, auditor, pentest, Vanta, and NDA availability | release-blocking until external proof is attached or copy is demoted to local/under-review scope |
| workflows | 11 workflow files present | workflow security needs generated permissions, action pinning, secrets, artifact, SBOM, and attestation audit |
| workflow security signals | `sbom.yml` emits CycloneDX/SPDX fallback; `lint.yml` runs `npm audit`; several workflows reference secrets and `uses: actions/checkout@v4`, `actions/setup-node@v4`, `actions/github-script@v7`, `softprops/action-gh-release@v2` | positive supply-chain work exists, but V3 requires explicit permission/trigger/pinning/attestation status for every workflow |
| API key doctor probes | both candidate keys make local doctor return `ok=true`, `blockers=0`, while cloud validation is unknown due fetch failure | local credential syntax/config proof exists; server-side production validation does not |
| credential model | `src/auth.js` stores tenant API keys as hashes, rejects query-param API keys, supports bearer/header/cookie auth, rotates tenant key, and redacts readiness outputs in many places | good foundation, but multi-key/scopes/session claims require one canonical contract and proof |
| OAuth surface | `src/oauth.js` mounts provider discovery/start/callback and tests prove route docs/OpenAPI visibility | OAuth route presence is not enough; state, nonce/PKCE policy, redirect allowlist, token redaction, and prod callback smoke are still required |

##### Node 10.1 External Trust Baseline V3

| baseline | official source | Kolm V3 requirement |
|---|---|---|
| OWASP API Security Top 10 2023 | https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0x00-header/ | every sensitive route must have object-level, property-level, and function-level authorization evidence |
| OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025 | https://owasp.org/www-project-top-10-for-large-language-model-applications/ | capture, distill, agent/tool, poisoning, prompt extraction, model theft, and unsafe output controls must be mapped and tested |
| NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile AI 600-1 | https://www.nist.gov/publications/artificial-intelligence-risk-management-framework-generative-artificial-intelligence | AI security/compliance evidence should map risks to govern, map, measure, and manage controls with dates and owners |
| OpenID Connect Core 1.0 | https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0-final.html | OIDC/SSO work needs claims, issuer/audience, nonce, redirect, token, and session semantics, not only OAuth links |
| SCIM 2.0 protocol | https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7644 | SCIM must be either standard Users/Groups provisioning or visibly metadata-only/partial |
| WebAuthn Level 3 | https://www.w3.org/TR/webauthn-3/ | MFA/passkey claims require real enrollment, challenge, recovery, enforcement, and audit proof |
| RFC 6750 bearer token usage | https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6750 | Kolm API keys are bearer secrets and must not appear in URLs, logs, screenshots, public docs, or release packets |
| OAuth 2.0 Security BCP RFC 9700 | https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9700 | OAuth/OIDC flows need redirect safety, replay resistance, PKCE or documented confidential-client exception, and token-leak tests |
| HHS HIPAA Security Rule | https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/security/index.html | HIPAA copy must distinguish implemented safeguards, BAA availability, PHI paths, and external legal status |
| European Commission GDPR guidance | https://commission.europa.eu/law/law-topic/data-protection_en | GDPR copy must map data subject rights, processor role, DPA/SCC, subprocessors, deletion/export, and residency |

##### Node 10.2 Security Control Contract V3

Every sensitive route, page, command, worker, or generated artifact should be representable by this contract:

```json
{
  "schema": "kolm.security_control_contract.v3",
  "owner_node": 10,
  "surface": null,
  "path_or_command": null,
  "data_classes": [],
  "auth": {
    "mode": "anonymous|api_key|session|oauth|sso|scim|admin|service",
    "public_allowlist_reason": null,
    "step_up_required": false,
    "session_policy": null
  },
  "authorization": {
    "tenant_fence": "required|not_applicable",
    "object_owner_check": null,
    "role_or_scope": [],
    "property_filter": [],
    "negative_tests": []
  },
  "secrets": {
    "inputs": [],
    "outputs": [],
    "storage": "none|hash|encrypted|external_ref|cookie|local_dev_only",
    "secret_values_included": false,
    "rotation": null,
    "leak_response": null
  },
  "privacy": {
    "retention": null,
    "redaction": null,
    "export": null,
    "delete": null,
    "residency": null
  },
  "audit": {
    "event_name": null,
    "fields": ["actor", "tenant", "target", "action", "result", "request_id", "timestamp"],
    "exportable": true,
    "siem_shape": null
  },
  "compliance_claim": {
    "status": "implemented|available_under_agreement|needs_live_certification|external_blocked|not_claimed",
    "evidence_paths": [],
    "public_copy_paths": [],
    "claim_scope_test": null
  },
  "proof": {
    "unit_tests": [],
    "integration_tests": [],
    "local_smoke": null,
    "production_smoke": null,
    "account_screenshot": null,
    "release_gate": null
  }
}
```

##### Node 10.3 Claim Scope Rules V3

Public and account copy must use one of these states:

| state | allowed language | forbidden language without proof |
|---|---|---|
| `implemented_control` | "control implemented", "local evidence available", "export template available" | "certified", "attested", "audited", "report available" |
| `agreement_available` | "BAA/DPA template available", "legal review required", "available on request after execution" | "signed for you", "covered" without customer-specific agreement |
| `auditor_ready` | "packet prepared", "controls mapped", "readiness package generated" | "SOC 2 attested", "ISO certified" |
| `live_certified` | exact certification/report language with auditor/report/date evidence | broader criteria, future dates, or unverified controls |
| `external_blocked` | "blocked on auditor/report/package/registry/partner" | any production-complete or certification-complete copy |
| `not_claimed` | no claim | hinting by logos, badges, or trust chips |

Current V3 state: compliance packet tests say live certification is not verified, so `SOC 2 Type I attested` on `public/security.html` is not allowed unless the auditor report evidence is attached and the readiness ledger is promoted.

##### Node 10.4 Credential And Session Rules V3

1. API keys are bearer secrets; production UX must prefer httpOnly sessions for browser account use and avoid long-lived raw keys in localStorage.
2. Query-string API keys stay rejected; tests must cover both valid and invalid bearer/header/cookie paths.
3. Key creation and rotation must show the secret once, never inside browser alerts, analytics, logs, screenshots, markdown reports, or copied release packets.
4. Multi-key/scoped-key UI requires real per-key backend rows and enforced middleware scopes. Otherwise UI must say "primary tenant key."
5. Provider/storage/OAuth/Stripe/webhook secrets require external secret-manager or encrypted/ref-only profile for production.
6. Readiness endpoints must display configured/missing/provider class and always prove secret values are absent.
7. Any key pasted into chat, logs, screenshots, issue trackers, docs, or reports must trigger rotation guidance and incident audit.
8. OAuth/OIDC sign-in must prove state, return URL safety, callback origin, redirect allowlist, token redaction, cookie flags, and production callback smoke.
9. SSO/SCIM/MFA/passkey copy must be demoted unless full route behavior, account UX, and tests exist.
10. Secret scanners must cover source, generated HTML, reports, screenshots, workflows, docs, examples, and artifacts.

##### Node 10.5 Authorization And Tenant Isolation Matrix V3

| protected object | required negative tests |
|---|---|
| account profile and settings | wrong tenant cannot read, edit, export, or delete |
| API keys and receipt secrets | wrong tenant/admin cannot list, rotate, prune, or infer prefixes |
| teams, invites, roles, approvals | wrong member, wrong role, expired invite, reused invite, and removed member all fail |
| captures and privacy lake | wrong tenant cannot read tail, stats, events, labels, or exports |
| artifacts and receipts | wrong tenant cannot inspect private artifact, verify private receipt metadata, or mutate provenance |
| distill/train/eval jobs | wrong tenant cannot create against another dataset, read logs, promote, cancel, or delete |
| billing, invoices, chargeback | wrong tenant cannot view usage, payment state, invoice metadata, or chargeback rows |
| provider and storage credentials | wrong tenant cannot view readiness beyond redacted provider class or mutate config |
| admin and diagnostics | non-admin cannot use tenant admin, debug, logs, secret readiness, or monitoring snapshots |
| marketplace and registry | publisher, reviewer, buyer, anonymous user, and admin roles are separated |

##### Node 10.6 Supply-Chain Security Fit V3

Node `10` owns the security requirements; node `22` owns the supply-chain system; node `9` owns release proof. The security side of the contract requires:

1. all workflows declare `permissions:` explicitly and justify write scopes;
2. third-party actions are SHA-pinned or covered by an allowlist with update cadence;
3. secret use is guarded, not logged, and not available to forked/untrusted paths;
4. SBOM generation is release-blocking when packages are produced;
5. artifact attestations are generated and verified for package, installer, and generated release artifacts;
6. dependency review, `npm audit`, SAST, and secret scanning results are attached to release evidence;
7. release notes state whether artifacts are attested, signed, SBOM-backed, or local-only.

##### Node 10.7 Work Tickets V3

| ticket | priority | node branch | required outcome | done proof |
|---|---|---:|---|---|
| W-SECURITY-V3-001 | P0 | 10.0 | Generate `docs/security-control-ledger.v3.json`. | Every route/page/command/workflow/module with security relevance has control class, owner, data class, auth mode, secret policy, audit event, and tests. |
| W-SECURITY-V3-002 | P0 | 10.1 | Generate route auth and public-allowlist matrix. | Public routes have reasons; private routes have auth mode and negative tests. |

| W-SECURITY-V3-009 | P0 | 10.4 | Bind redaction benchmark to privacy UI and public claims. | Class coverage, fixture date, precision/recall/F1, false positives, and limits are visible. |

| W-SECURITY-V3-018 | P0 | 10.9 | Build account trust console from live contracts. | Account UI shows implemented controls, missing proof, credential state, privacy state, readiness, evidence freshness, and export path. |

##### Node 10 V3 Security Redline

Node `10` is not 100 percent complete until:

1. claim-scope verification passes with no unbacked SOC 2, ISO, HIPAA, GDPR, DPA, BAA, FedRAMP, penetration-test, Vanta, or auditor-report language;
2. the compliance packet's `live_certification_verified=false` state is reflected everywhere until external proof closes the blockers;
3. every security-sensitive route has a generated control record and negative authorization tests;
4. every credential class has generation, storage, transport, display, rotation, revocation, leak response, and audit evidence;
5. browser account UX does not depend on long-lived raw production API keys in localStorage;
6. SSO, SCIM, MFA/passkey, OAuth/OIDC, and API-key scopes are either fully implemented and tested or visibly demoted;
7. privacy/redaction evidence includes data class, retention, export, deletion, residency, and benchmark scope;
8. every trust/security/compliance public page renders from evidence state or is checked by a claim-scope gate;
9. workflow and supply-chain security evidence is attached to the release packet;
10. production security smoke proves the deployed commit with environment-provided keys and no secret leakage.

### Core AI Product Capability Ledger V1

This branch maintains the actual product spine. The website, docs, account, backend, and roadmap must all explain the same three product promises:

1. capture and route model traffic without locking into one provider;
2. compile, distill, evaluate, and sign owned `.kolm` artifacts from that traffic and other datasets;
3. run enterprise AI artifacts across hosted, cloud, local, browser, mobile, edge, and air-gapped targets.

If any page only sells "one AI API" or only sells "AI compiler", it is incomplete. Kolm is the evidence-to-artifact operating system for production AI: capture behavior, learn from it, prove it, package it, and run it anywhere.

#### Current Product Graph Evidence

| graph item | current value | completion implication |
|---|---:|---|
| journeys | 12 | every product promise must map to journey-level account, CLI, TUI, API, proof, and docs |
| route surfaces | 7 | every route group must have product-surface ownership |
| route groups | 163 | route ownership is large enough to require generated governance |
| total routes | 582 | all public product copy must be backed by this route graph or a closeout gate |
| product-graph API routes | 69 | the graph is intentionally summarized; completion requires cross-checking with the full route ledger |
| CLI commands | 64 | every core product journey needs a CLI proof path |
| TUI views | 32 | every operator journey needs a text-mode path for local, air-gapped, and server environments |
| account links | 33 | post-auth UX must show the whole product matrix, not only billing/settings |
| readiness requirements | 57 | product claims must map to shipped, implemented, or explicit external/package/benchmark/certification gates |
| open closeout requirements | 8 | public benchmark data, live certification, package release, and external ecosystem adoption remain non-local proof gates |

#### State-Of-Art Research Anchors For This Branch

| area | primary source | product implication |
|---|---|---|
| compiler IR | MLIR describes a multi-level IR that can represent, analyze, and transform high-level dataflow graphs down to target-specific code: https://mlir.llvm.org/docs/LangRef/ | `.kolm` compile should have a typed IR, verifier, pass pipeline, textual dump, and target lowering story rather than ad hoc artifact assembly |
| retargetable ML compiler/runtime | IREE is an MLIR-based compiler and runtime designed to scale from datacenter to mobile and edge deployments: https://iree.dev/ | "run anywhere" should learn from IREE-style ahead-of-time compilation, runtime packaging, device constraints, and benchmark discipline |
| ML graph optimization | Apache TVM Relax is a graph abstraction for optimization/transformation and cross-level optimization with TensorIR: https://tvm.apache.org/docs/deep_dive/relax/index.html | Kolm should expose graph passes, fusion/decomposition decisions, dynamic-shape constraints, and target-specific compilation reports |
| portable inference | ONNX Runtime GenAI documents APIs for running generative AI models with ONNX Runtime: https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/genai/ | runtime export should include ONNX Runtime compatibility, execution provider notes, and failure modes |
| knowledge distillation | Hinton, Vinyals, and Dean define distillation as compressing ensemble/model knowledge into a deployable model: https://arxiv.org/abs/1503.02531 | distillation UX must show teacher, student, temperature/objective, task coverage, holdout, and deployment cost benefit |
| quantization | GPTQ uses approximate second-order information for efficient post-training quantization of GPT-style models: https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.17323 | quantization oracle should justify method choice and calibration sample size, not just output a bit width |
| activation-aware quantization | AWQ is a hardware-friendly low-bit weight-only quantization method that uses activation awareness: https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.00978 | device/runtime fit should track activation sensitivity and runtime kernel support |
| activation smoothing | SmoothQuant shifts quantization difficulty from activations to weights to enable W8A8 LLM quantization: https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.10438 | compiler reports should expose outlier handling and whether activation quantization is actually supported on target hardware |
| serving memory | PagedAttention/vLLM improves throughput by addressing KV-cache fragmentation and sharing: https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.06180 | runtime placement, ITKV, and serving claims need KV-cache accounting, batch/latency curves, and long-context tests |
| speculative inference | speculative decoding uses a faster draft model whose tokens are verified by a target model: https://huggingface.co/papers/2211.17192 | student-as-draft and speculative routes must prove acceptance rate, correctness, rollback, and latency deltas |

#### Product UX Contract For Core Capability

Using `ui-ux-pro-max`, the account and public product surfaces should not make users infer the product matrix from paragraphs. Each core journey must expose:

1. one-line outcome, not jargon;
2. current implementation status and proof freshness;
3. primary action with one obvious next step;
4. live state from API where post-auth;
5. empty state that creates data or connects a provider;
6. error state that explains missing credentials, unsupported device, missing model, failed eval, or external gate;
7. cost, latency, privacy, and quality tradeoff before irreversible work;
8. target runtime/device selector where artifacts are involved;
9. holdout/eval/verifier before promotion;
10. downloadable or copyable proof artifact after completion;
11. no hover-only disclosure, no hidden route contract, no static fake cards;
12. clean relation between public page, docs guide, account page, CLI command, TUI view, and API route.

#### Core Journey Completion Gates

| journey | done means |
|---|---|
| gateway capture | OpenAI, Anthropic, OpenRouter, Gemini-compatible, and self-hosted OpenAI-compatible flows are captured or explicitly scoped; RBAC, redaction, zero-retention, tailing, review, and promotion all have fixtures |
| privacy lake | capture storage, redaction, opt-in/out, deletion, export, tail, trends, and repeated-workflow discovery are real, consent-aware, and visible in account |
| datasets and labeling | candidate generation, label queue, synthetic data, holdout split, dataset export, and promotion are linked to distill/eval gates |
| train and distill | teacher/student/backbone, compute target, strategy, cost quote, holdout, telemetry, artifact output, and diagnostics are all visible and testable |
| models and backbones | model catalog includes license, source, freshness, modality, VRAM, runtime support, device fit, and recommendation reasoning |
| multimodal tokenization | image/audio/video/PDF/text/code sidecars are generated with local fallback, redaction, provenance, and compile readiness |
| compile and verify | spec-to-artifact pipeline has typed IR, target selection, quantization report, signature, provenance, diff, lineage, offline verify, and export |
| runtime inference | hosted/local/browser/edge/device serving supports fallback chains, streaming where claimed, telemetry, package readiness, and runtime adoption scope |
| compute and cloud | object storage, GPU/trainer, BYOC, remote SSH, managed train, broker, deploy plan, and cloud job lifecycle are all provable without secrets leaking |
| devices and fleet | detect, recommend, register, test, install, uninstall, tunnel, and airgap flows work across declared device classes |
| enterprise governance | billing, keys, audit, SSO/SCIM/MFA, compliance package, capture RBAC, team roles, and claim scope are live and evidence-backed |
| agents and registry | MCP export, agent telemetry, verified publisher policy, marketplace, and replayable run logs are aligned with artifact provenance |
